program: Business English — Executive Communication for International Leadership
hub_subtitle: Business English Executivo para Reuniões Internacionais e Liderança

lesson.menu_title: The CEO Introducing Herself on a Thursday Call
lesson.menu_desc: Present simple, frequency adverbs, and how the weekly call runs -- 28 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 2 -- The CEO Introducing Herself on a Thursday Call
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Izabel Cristina de Pieri | Aula 2 -- The Thursday Call

listenings.text: Hi Izabel, it's Mark from the supplier side. I'm just leaving you a quick update before Thursday. We usually ship the components in two weeks, but this order is a little bigger, so it normally takes about three. I always send you the tracking numbers by email, and they should reach you tomorrow. One more thing: we've streamlined our order process, so from now on you can approve everything in one place. If you have any questions, you know where to find me. Talk soon, and have a good week.

Good morning, team. Just a short briefing before the weekly call. As you know, Izabel runs the Thursday meeting, and she usually opens with the cash flow and then the operations update. This week, please make sure your numbers are ready by Wednesday evening. I oversee the reporting side, so I ensure that every figure is correct before it goes to the board. We often get questions from the new partners, so keep your summaries clear and short. Thank you, everyone, and let's make this a strong meeting.

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: to make a process simpler and more efficient
to watch over work and make sure it is done well
to be in charge of running the daily business
to guide and take responsibility for a group of people
to follow the progress or status of something
to make certain that something happens
to deal with a task, a client, or a problem
to stay in contact and work closely with someone

reading.rtitle: How Izabel Runs the Thursday Call
paras: Every Thursday, Izabel runs the weekly meeting with the American partners. She is the CEO of AVRT, and she leads a small team in Valinhos. The call always follows the same shape. She usually opens with a short update on the cash flow, then she moves to operations. On her end, she oversees the whole business, so she ensures that the numbers are ready before the board sees them.

Izabel rarely reads from a script. Instead, she tracks the key figures during the week and liaises with her suppliers so that nothing is a surprise on Thursday. Her team handles the invoices and the day-to-day tasks, and she often streamlines a process when she sees a bottleneck. 'A good meeting is a boring meeting,' she likes to say. 'If I prepare well, there are no bad surprises.'

The partners frequently ask her difficult questions, and she does not always have every answer at her fingertips. When that happens, she does not freeze. She simply says, 'Let me check that and get back to you.' That short sentence, she believes, is one of the most useful things an executive can learn to say in English.
reading.source: Adapted from an executive communication feature, The Leadership Brief

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: Izabel dislikes the Thursday meeting and wants to cancel it.
Izabel prepares carefully so that the weekly call runs smoothly and without surprises.
Izabel reads everything from a script during the call.

items: Izabel runs the Thursday call with the American partners.
'Every Thursday, Izabel runs the weekly meeting with the American partners.'
She usually starts the call with the operations update.
She usually opens with the cash flow, then moves to operations.
She tracks the key figures during the week so there are no surprises.
'she tracks the key figures during the week and liaises with her suppliers'
When she does not know an answer, she freezes and stops talking.
She says, 'Let me check that and get back to you.'

parts: with the cash flow, and she
the whole business. During the week she
with her suppliers, so there are no surprises.

gapfill.text: Notice the present simple: for he/she/it we add -s (she runs, she opens, she oversees, she tracks, she liaises). Frequency adverbs (always, usually, often, rarely) go BEFORE the main verb (she usually opens) but AFTER the verb 'be' (she is always ready).

lf.title: Where does the frequency adverb go?

lf.text: The frequency adverb goes BEFORE the main verb (she usually opens, they frequently ask, she rarely reads), but AFTER the verb 'be' (she is always ready). Sentence C uses 'be', so 'always' comes after it.

bank.label: Useful language for opening and running the call
items: Good morning, everyone. Let me give you a quick update on...
Just to bring everyone up to speed...
On my end, this week we...
I usually start with the cash flow, and then...
Let me walk you through the numbers.
That's a good question. Let me check that and get back to you.
To wrap up, our main priorities this week are...
I oversee the... and my team handles the...
A new partner asks what you do at AVRT. Describe your role using the present simple: what you oversee, run, and lead.
Describe your typical Thursday call: three things you usually do, using frequency adverbs (usually, always, often).
A partner asks a question you cannot answer right now. Respond professionally without freezing.
Open the weekly call for the new partners: greet them, say what you will cover, and give a one-line update on the cash flow.

answerkey.title: Reveal the answer key
list: Gap-fill: 1 = runs, 2 = opens, 3 = oversees, 4 = tracks, 5 = liaises.
Model role (present simple): 'I run operations at AVRT. I oversee the whole business and I lead a small team in Valinhos.'
Frequency adverbs: before the main verb (I usually open with the cash flow), but after 'be' (I am always ready before the call).
When you don't know an answer: 'That's a good question. Let me check that and get back to you.'
answerkey.note: Remember the -s in the third person: she runs, she opens, she oversees, she tracks, she liaises.
